---
title: "Workflow Management"
subtitle: "Automating multi-step computational pipelines"
author: "Samuel Pawel, Peter Degen, Fabio Molo, Gorka Fraga González"
date-format: long
preview-links: true
include-in-header:
  - text: |
      <style>
      #title-slide .title {
        font-size: 1.8em;
        color: #29487d;
      }
      </style>
format:
  revealjs:
    menu: true
    pdf: true
    code-line-numbers: false
    smaller: true
    fig-align: left
    logo: "img/uzh_logo_d_pos_web_assoc.jpg"
    embed-resources: true
    progress: true
    slide-number: true
    footer: "Center for Reproducible Science and Research Synthesis | [Home](../../index.qmd)"
    title-slide-attributes:
      data-background-size: contain
      data-background-position: bottom
      data-background-opacity: "0.8"
bibliography: references.bib
nocite: |
  @Peikert2021
---

## The problem: multi-step pipelines

::: incremental
-   A typical research project has **multiple scripts** that must run in order:
    -   `download.R` → `clean.R` → `analyse.R` → `figures.R` → `paper.qmd`

-   How to know **which files to run in which order**?

-   How to avoid **re-running everything** when only one file changed?

-   How to make the pipeline **self-documenting and shareable**?

. . .

**Solution: workflow management tools (e.g., Make, Snakemake, targets)**
:::

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Automation with Make

-   Tool for managing **computational recipes**

-   **Automating multiple steps** into one

. . .

:::::: columns
::: {.column width="50%"}
> Arrabbiata sauce, or sugo all'arrabbiata in Italian, is a spicy sauce for pasta made from garlic, tomatoes, and dried red chili peppers cooked in olive oil.

[https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Arrabbiata_sauce](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Arrabbiata_sauce){style="font-size:0.7em"}
:::

:::: {.column width="50%"}
::: fragment
Makefile
``` makefile
arrabiata.pdf: arrabiata.qmd sauce.csv pasta.R
  quarto render arrabiata.qmd

sauce.csv: cook.R tomatoes.zip aromatics.yaml
  R -e "source(cook.R)"

aromatics.yaml: sizzle.py garlic.txt chili.json
  python sizzle.py garlic.txt
  python sizzle.py chili.json
```

Terminal
``` bash
make arrabiata.pdf # execute recipe
```
:::
::::
::::::

::: fragment
![](img/Make-diagram.png){fig-align="center" width="600"}
:::

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## How Make works

::: incremental
-   A **Makefile** defines **targets**, **dependencies**, and **recipes**

-   Make checks **timestamps**: only re-runs a target if a dependency is newer

-   **Dependency graph** ensures correct execution order automatically

-   Running `make` from the terminal executes the minimal set of steps needed
:::

::: fragment
``` makefile
# Pattern: target: dependencies
#              recipe (must be indented with a TAB)

report.html: analysis.R data.csv paper.qmd
    Rscript analysis.R
    quarto render paper.qmd
```
:::

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## A minimal research Makefile

``` makefile
# Final target
all: paper.pdf

# Render the paper (depends on cleaned data and figures)
paper.pdf: paper.qmd results/estimates.rds figures/fig1.png
    quarto render paper.qmd --to pdf

# Run statistical analysis
results/estimates.rds: R/analyse.R data/clean.csv
    Rscript R/analyse.R

# Generate figure 1
figures/fig1.png: R/figures.R results/estimates.rds
    Rscript R/figures.R

# Clean raw data
data/clean.csv: R/clean.R data/raw.csv
    Rscript R/clean.R

# Remove all generated files
clean:
    rm -rf paper.pdf results/ figures/
```
$


------------------------------------------------------------------------

## CRS Primer on Workflow Management

:::::: columns
::: {.column width="45%"}
![<https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.15792190>](img/primer-make.png)
:::
::::::

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Additional Resources

-   GNU Make manual: <https://www.gnu.org/software/make/manual/>
-   The `targets` R package book: <https://books.ropensci.org/targets/>
-   Snakemake documentation: <https://snakemake.readthedocs.io>
-   The Turing Way — Reproducible Pipelines: <https://the-turing-way.netlify.app/reproducible-research/make>
-   @Peikert2021: a full reproducible workflow combining Make, Git, and Docker

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## References {.smaller}
